sanskrit_devanagari:
मनुष्याणां सहस्रेषु कश्चिद्यतति सिद्धये ।
यततामपि सिद्धानां कश्चिन्मां वेत्ति तत्त्वतः ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
manuṣyāṇāṁ sahasreṣu kaścid yatati siddhaye |
yatatām api siddhānāṁ kaścin māṁ vetti tattvataḥ || 3 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.003 = MBh_06,029.003). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: of those who have attained perfection

translations.verbatim_quote: Out of many thousands among men, one may endeavor for perfection, and of those who have achieved perfection, hardly one knows Me in truth.
Amongst thousands of persons, hardly one strives for perfection; and amongst those who have achieved perfection, hardly one knows Me in truth.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are various grades of men, and out of many thousands, one may be sufficiently interested in transcendental realization to try to know what is the self, what is the body, and what is the Absolute Truth.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.3 Sahasreṣu manuṣyāṇām, among thousands, among a multitude of men; kaścit, a rare one; yatati, endeavours; siddhaye, for perfection. [For perfection: for the rise of Knowledge through the purification of the mind.] Siddhānām api, even of the perfected; yatatām, who are diligent.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads siddhi as the rise of Knowledge through mind-purification. Even among those who have purified mind and attained that siddhi, only a rare one knows Krishna tattvataḥ — as the principle. Two layers of rarity, both structural.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.3 'Men', i.e., those who are qualified for observing the commands of the Sastras — among thousands of such men, only some one strives till the attainment of perfection. Among thousands who strive till the attainment of perfection, some one only, understanding Me, strives to attain success.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja narrows the population at each stage. The 'thousands' is already restricted to those qualified to follow the śāstras. Even among the perfected, the one who knows Krishna in truth has understood that Krishna is the goal — not just attained the technique.

positions.rendering: one may endeavor ... hardly one knows Me in truth
hardly one strives ... hardly one knows Me in truth
two layers of rarity (rare striver, rarer truth-knower); siddhi = Knowledge-rise via mind purification
population already restricted to śāstra-qualified; among them rare strivers; among strivers, the one who knows Krishna is the goal

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators agree on the structural shape — double rarity, two stages — but differ on what siddhi (perfection) means. Shankara reads siddhi as the rise of Knowledge through mind-purification (Advaita's standard schema). Ramanuja reads siddhi as the perfection of action-discipline that prepares for the recognition of Krishna as goal. The Vaishnava translators (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) follow the Vishishtadvaita reading. The shared claim across all four readings is that the verse's rarity is structural, not motivational — there is no soft reading.

literal_meaning: Among thousands of men, hardly one strives for perfection; and among those who have attained perfection, hardly one knows Me in truth.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's structural-rarity verse. Krishna names two layers of filtering: (1) among the population at large, only a rare individual strives for siddhi at all; (2) among those who attain siddhi, only a rare one knows Krishna tattvataḥ — as the principle. The rarity is structural, not motivational; both halves are filters, and both filters are sharp. The verse is not flattering to a mass audience, and it is not meant to be — it sets the stakes for what the chapter will reveal next.
